description: This bar graph quantifies the individual contributions of five strengthening mechanisms—dislocations, grain boundaries, ordering, solid solution, and lattice frictional stress—to the yield strength (YS) of Fe-6.8Al ferritic low-density steel under three processing conditions: 98% cold rolling (CR), and isothermal annealing at 750 °C for 5 min and 60 min. The 98% CR state exhibits the highest YS of 1800 MPa, dominated by dislocation strengthening and grain boundary hardening. After annealing at 750 °C for 5 min, the YS drops to 700 MPa, primarily due to a drastic reduction in dislocation density, while contributions from grain boundaries and nano-sized ordered Fe3Al (DO3) phases remain significant. Prolonged annealing for 60 min further reduces the YS to 265 MPa, as all strengthening contributions diminish substantially owing to grain growth and extended recovery. The comparison demonstrates that the ultrahigh strength in the cold-rolled condition arises mainly from work-hardening via dislocations, whereas controlled short-term annealing retains useful strength through grain boundary and ordered-phase strengthening while improving ductility.
